{"content": {"text": "[<context summary>
- The context belongs to this section: Configure privacy settings
- The context belongs to this subsection: Masking overrides
- Keywords: masking, overrides, iOS, Android, New Relic
- About the context: This snippet provides details on how to apply masking overrides in the Mobile Session Replay feature of New Relic, including examples for iOS and Android platforms. It explains the different methods available for applying overrides, such as inline attributes, local API methods, and future server-side rules. This is part of the larger document that describes the overall Mobile Session Replay functionality and configuration.
</context summary>

<context body>
Example of adding an `accessibilityIdentifier`\: ~~~~~~~~~~ let myView = UIView() myView.accessibilityIdentifier = "nr-mask" ~~~~~~~~~~ Example of appending an existing `accessibilityIdentifier`\: ~~~~~~~~~~ let myView = UIView() myView.accessibilityIdentifier = "someExistingId.nr-mask" ~~~~~~~~~~ #### Android - XML layouts Add an `nr-mask` or `nr-unmask` tag to the view you'd like to mask or unmask. Example: ~~~~~~~~~~ <EditText android:id="@+id/editTextEmail" android:layout_width="390dp" android:inputType="text" android:tag="nr-unmask" android:importantForAutofill="no" /> ~~~~~~~~~~ ~~~~~~~~~~ <EditText android:id="@+id/editTextEmail" android:layout_width="390dp" android:inputType="text" android:tag="nr-mask" android:importantForAutofill="no" /> ~~~~~~~~~~ ### Local API methods Local API method masking overrides are also applied in your code within the New Relic agent configuration. They are useful when the targeted views already have unique identifiers and you want to manage them in a centralized list instead of tagging each view individually throughout your code. #### iOS - UIKit You can mask or unmask views by adding their classes or accessibilityIdentifiers to the following methods: 1. `addSessionReplayMaskViewClass` 2. `addSessionReplayUnmaskViewClass` 3. `addSessionReplayMaskViewAccessibilityIdentifier` 4. `addSessionReplayUnmaskViewAccessibilityIdentifier` Example: ~~~~~~~~~~ NewRelic.addSessionReplayMaskViewClass("SecureLabel") NewRelic.addSessionReplayUnmaskViewClass("UnsecureLabel") NewRelic.addSessionReplayMaskedAccessibilityIdentifier("login") NewRelic.addSessionReplayUnmaskedAccessibilityIdentifier("editTextEmail") ~~~~~~~~~~ #### Android - XML layouts You can mask or unmask views by adding their classes or android:tags to the following methods: 1. `addSessionReplayMaskViewClass` 2. `addSessionReplayUnmaskViewClass` 3. `addSessionReplayMaskViewTag` 4. `addSessionReplayUnaskViewTag` Example: ~~~~~~~~~~ NewRelic.addSessionReplayMaskViewTag("login"); NewRelic.addSessionReplayUnmaskViewTag("editTextEmail"); NewRelic.addSessionReplayUnmaskViewClass("android.widget.Button"); NewRelic.addSessionReplayMaskViewClass("android.widget.TextView"); NewRelic.addSessionReplayMaskViewClass("com.yourpackagename.CustomView"); ~~~~~~~~~~ ### Server-side rules Server-side rules are not yet supported in the Limited Preview of Mobile Session Replay. This documentation is provided for informational purposes only, to allow you to prepare for when it becomes available. TODO: Finish this section. Applying server-side masking rules allows you to retroactively patch masking mistakes in app versions that have already been released. They take immediate effect on all new session starts. ## Enforcement priority of conflicting masking rules If multiple masking rules apply to a single view or element, the following rules, in priority order, determine which takes precedence. ### 1. Strategy supersedes everything If your masking strategy is Default, modes and overrides are not enforced. For example, if you have added inline `nr-unmask` attribute overrides in your code but the Default masking strategy is selected in settings, everything will still be masked. ### 2. Overrides supercede modes Modes determine the standard behavior when an override isn't present, so if an override is present, it will always take precedence over the mode. For example, if "Mask application text" mode is enabled and you add an inline `nr-unmask` attribute override to a view in your code, that view will be unmasked but every other view will be masked. ### 3. Masking overrides supersede unmasking overrides If a single view has both masking and unmasking overrides, it will always be masked. Applying server-side masking rules allows you to retroactively patch masking mistakes in app versions that have already been released. They take immediate effect on all new session starts. For example, if a view has an inline `nr-unmask` attribute override and an `nr-mask` server-side rule, the view will be masked. # View Mobile session replays in New Relic Once you start collecting session replays, they can be used to improve your digital experiences by identifying common patterns and areas where users experience friction. They're also helpful to quickly diagnose and resolve issues by reviewing the steps users took before encountering errors or unexpected behavior. Replays can be viewed in two places which have been optimized for these use cases: ## On the Session Replay page The Session Replay page provides a list of all replays that were collected over your selected time period. By default, we filter the list to replays that lasted at least 5 seconds. The table provides the helpful data points like error counts and the filters allow you to narrow the list to sessions to those that will help solve your problem. For example, you can add a `countryCode` filter to see replays from a specific region to validate localization is working as expected or a `deviceModel` filter to see replays from a specific model of phone that may have compatibility issues. To access the Session replay page: 1. Go to **one.newrelic.com > All Capabilities** **> Mobile**. 2. Select your mobile app 3. In the left-hand menu, scroll down and click **Session replay**. 4. Click on a row to view the replay ## On the Crashes, Request errors, Handled exceptions, and ANRs pages Replays in the Crashes and ANRs experiences are not yet supported in the Limited Preview of Mobile Session Replay. This documentation is provided for informational purposes only, to allow you to prepare for when they become available later in the preview period. If a user encountered an issue while replay was being captured, a preview of the replay will be available on the error occurrence in the Crashes](https://docs.newrelic.com/docs/errors-inbox/mobile-tab/), [Request errors](https://docs.newrelic.com/docs/errors-inbox/group-request-errors/), [Handles exceptions](https://docs.newrelic.com/docs/mobile-monitoring/mobile-monitoring-ui/crashes/handled-exceptions-analyze-trends-prevent-crashes/), and [ANRs experiences. The preview shows a 5-10 seconds leading up to the issue and, for non-fatal issues, 5-10 seconds after it occurred. An icon is overlaid on the timeline to show you exactly when the occurred in the replay. This allows you to quickly assess any user actions that may have caused the issue, as well as the severity of the impact to the end-user. 
</context body>]", "type": "TEXT"}, "location": {"confluenceLocation": {"url": "https://newrelic.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/APPEXP/pages/4476862587/MSR+Docs+-+LP+-+Initial+draft"}, "type": "CONFLUENCE"}, "score": 0.6042051}

{"content": {"text": "[<context summary>
- The context belongs to this section: Sentry and Dynatrace
- The context belongs to this subsection: Sentry
- Keywords: session replay, native view hierarchy, SwiftUI, privacy, masking
- About the context: This snippet describes Sentry's approach to mobile session replay, specifically for the SwiftUI framework. It explains that Sentry uses a native view hierarchy capture method to provide high-fidelity session replays. The snippet also covers Sentry's approach to privacy and data masking for SwiftUI, noting that while Sentry provides aggressive default masking, the documentation lacks details on SwiftUI-specific APIs for more granular control.
</context summary>

<context body>
Session Replay is not its core product but a powerful supplementary feature. Its primary use case is to provide immediate visual context for an error or crash report, allowing developers to see the exact sequence of user actions that led to the issue, thereby drastically reducing debugging time.19 **Core Mobile Capture Architecture:** Sentry uses a Native View Hierarchy Capture method. Their documentation clarifies that a replay is not a video or screen recording but a "reconstruction" of the application's state.12 This is analogous to how their web replay product reconstructs the DOM. A GitHub issue discussing the feature's development confirms that the engineering goal was to directly support native frameworks like UIKit and SwiftUI, not lower-level graphics APIs like Metal, aligning it with this high-fidelity approach.26 **SwiftUI Capture Implementation & Technical Approach:** * **Instrumentation:** Sentry provides official support for SwiftUI through its unified Sentry Cocoa SDK (requiring version 8.31.1 or higher).27 The setup for session replay is integrated directly into the standard Sentry initialization configuration block, making it simple for existing Sentry users to enable. * **Capture Mechanism:** The SDK captures the application's state, which includes screen transitions, user gestures like taps and swipes, network requests, and other telemetry.27 The public documentation does not offer a deep dive into the specific technical mechanism used to traverse and capture the SwiftUI view hierarchy, focusing more on the configuration and features. * **Privacy & Data Masking:** Sentry's SDK employs an aggressive-by-default privacy posture, masking all text and images automatically. Developers have the option to disable these defaults (e.g., `maskAllText = false`) or follow a guide for custom masking to achieve more granular control.27 The documentation snippets do not mention any SwiftUI-specific modifiers like those found in LogRocket or Clarity, which may suggest that privacy controls rely more on global rules or class-based redaction rather than per-view modifiers. * **Limitations & Known Issues:** A potential limitation is the apparent lack of SwiftUI-specific APIs for granular control over masking and tagging. While global rules are powerful, complex applications often require the ability to redact specific, individual views, and it is unclear from the documentation how this is achieved in a pure SwiftUI context. **Primary Developer Documentation Link:** `https://docs.sentry.io/platforms/apple/guides/ios/session-replay/` 27 ### 6. Dynatrace **Provider Snapshot:** Dynatrace is an enterprise-grade, all-in-one observability platform that heavily emphasizes its AI engine, "Davis." Session Replay is a feature within its Digital Experience Monitoring (DEM) suite. Its primary purpose is to feed qualitative, visual data into the AI engine, which then correlates user-facing issues with back-end performance problems to provide automated root cause analysis.8 **Core Mobile Capture Architecture:** Dynatrace describes its mobile replay as an Event-Based Reconstruction. The documentation states that it is a "video-like reconstruction of the user interactions with mobile applications that use captured events and data".30 This phrasing suggests a high-fidelity approach that is more sophisticated than simple wireframes, but it also comes with a crucial caveat that replayed sessions can differ from the actual user experience. **SwiftUI Capture Implementation & Technical Approach:** * **Instrumentation:** SwiftUI is officially supported through the Dynatrace OneAgent for iOS (version 8.323 and higher).30 The instrumentation process is guided by a wizard within the Dynatrace web application, which is typical for enterprise-focused platforms. * **Capture Mechanism:** While the documentation confirms that SwiftUI is a supported technology, it lacks deep technical specifics on the capture mechanism itself.30 The admission that replays "can differ from the actual user experience" is a significant qualifier, suggesting potential gaps in fidelity, especially with complex or custom SwiftUI components.30 * **Privacy & Data Masking:** Dynatrace offers a tiered approach to privacy with three predefined masking levels: "Safest," "Safe," and "Custom," which can be configured globally. For more granular control, developers can define custom masking rules that target views based on their `accessibilityIdentifier` or a specific masking tag.30 However, it is not clear from the available documentation whether these identifier-based methods work reliably with SwiftUI's unique view rendering. * **Limitations & Known Issues:** The most notable limitation is the explicit statement that replays may not be 100% accurate, which could be a deal-breaker for teams needing to debug subtle visual bugs.30 Additionally, Dynatrace Session Replay is not available for any cross-platform frameworks like React Native or Flutter. **Primary Developer Documentation Link:** `https://docs.dynatrace.com/docs/observe/digital-experience/session-replay/session-replay-ios` 30 ### 7. Glassbox **Provider Snapshot:** Glassbox is an enterprise-focused digital experience analytics platform, with a particularly strong presence in the financial services and insurance industries. A core feature of its platform is "tagless data capture," which automatically records 100% of user interactions and technical events without requiring manual instrumentation, providing a complete dataset for analysis.8 **Core Mobile Capture Architecture:** Glassbox utilizes a Native View Hierarchy Capture method. The platform is designed to automatically capture "every user interaction and technical event" from native mobile apps and deliver "video-like session replays".32 The company emphasizes its lightweight and mature SDK, which is engineered for high performance and stability in large-scale deployments. **SwiftUI Capture Implementation & Technical Approach:** * **Instrumentation:** The Glassbox documentation boasts high compatibility with a wide range of mobile frameworks, including iOS, Android, React Native, and even modern declarative frameworks like Jetpack Compose.33 While SwiftUI is not explicitly named in the provided marketing materials, its support is highly probable given the platform's enterprise focus and support for analogous technologies. * **Capture Mechanism:** The specific technical approach for capturing SwiftUI views is not detailed in the available research material.34 * **Privacy & Data Masking:** Glassbox heavily promotes its advanced and robust privacy controls, highlighting "patent-pending data masking" and omitting capabilities that give enterprises fine-grained control over what data is captured and who can access it.33 However, the specific APIs or methods for applying these controls within a SwiftUI application are not described in the provided snippets. * **Limitations & Known Issues:** The most significant finding from the research is the lack of specific, public-facing developer documentation regarding their SwiftUI implementation. For a prospective customer's engineering team, this information gap makes it difficult to evaluate the maturity and technical fit of the Glassbox solution for a modern SwiftUI application.34 **Primary Developer Documentation Link:** `https://www.glassbox.com/platform/mobile-app-analytics/` 33 (Note: This is a high-level product page. Specific, detailed developer documentation for SwiftUI was not found in the provided research.) ### 8. Contentsquare **Provider Snapshot:** Contentsquare is another major enterprise platform in the experience analytics space, competing directly with Glassbox. It focuses on providing a comprehensive suite of tools to understand and optimize customer journeys. A key feature is its AI engine, "Sense," which can automatically analyze and summarize session replays to surface the most impactful moments of user friction or engagement.8 **Core Mobile Capture Architecture:** Contentsquare employs a Native Screen Content Capture method. 
</context body>]", "type": "TEXT"}, "location": {"confluenceLocation": {"url": "https://newrelic.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/APPEXP/pages/4655743612/SwiftUI+-+Wireframe+vs.+screenshots"}, "type": "CONFLUENCE"}, "score": 0.5974247}

{"content": {"text": "[<context summary>
- The context belongs to this section: Foundational Technologies in Mobile Session Replay
- The context belongs to this subsection: Method 3: The Native View Hierarchy & Draw Instruction Method
- Keywords: native rendering, draw instructions, pixel-perfect replay, performance, privacy
- About the context: This snippet describes Fullstory's advanced approach to mobile session replay capture, which uses a native view hierarchy and draw instruction capture method. This allows Fullstory to achieve pixel-perfect replay fidelity while maintaining high performance and strong privacy guarantees through on-device data masking. The snippet provides details on Fullstory's instrumentation, capture mechanism, and privacy controls for the SwiftUI framework.
</context summary>

<context body>
The company's documentation explicitly states that its SDK captures "draw instructions---not screenshots".4 This approach is positioned as a key differentiator, enabling high-fidelity, pixel-perfect replays while remaining lightweight and privacy-centric by design. **SwiftUI Capture Implementation & Technical Approach:** * **Instrumentation:** Fullstory claims "automatic instrumentation for SwiftUI," suggesting a highly integrated and seamless setup process for developers.4 The SDK is installed using standard dependency managers like Swift Package Manager or CocoaPods and requires the addition of a build phase script to handle the uploading of assets, which is a common pattern for mobile replay SDKs.18 * **Capture Mechanism:** The "automatic instrumentation" claim implies that Fullstory's SDK hooks deeply into SwiftUI's rendering lifecycle, allowing it to capture the view hierarchy and user interactions without requiring developers to manually tag each screen or control. While the provided documentation snippets do not detail the specific modifiers or APIs used, this approach suggests a more sophisticated and less intrusive integration than many competitors. * **Privacy & Data Masking:** A cornerstone of Fullstory's platform is its privacy-by-design architecture. For mobile, this is manifested in its on-device redaction capability. The SDK evaluates and redacts sensitive information directly on the user's device *before* any data is transmitted to Fullstory's servers. This ensures that personally identifiable information (PII) never leaves the application's secure sandbox, a significant security advantage over solutions that perform redaction on the server-side, which creates a potential window of exposure.4 * **Limitations & Known Issues:** The available research material did not contain detailed documentation on specific limitations or edge cases for Fullstory's SwiftUI implementation. A complete assessment would require access to their full developer documentation, which was not available.18 **Primary Developer Documentation Link:** `https://help.fullstory.com/hc/en-us/articles/360042772333-Getting-Started-with-iOS-Data-Capture` 18 ### 3. Datadog **Provider Snapshot:** Datadog is a dominant force in the cloud-scale monitoring and observability market. Session Replay is not a standalone product but is deeply integrated into its Real User Monitoring (RUM) solution. This allows organizations to connect front-end user behavior and visual replays directly with back-end performance metrics, traces, and logs, providing a unified view for troubleshooting.2 **Core Mobile Capture Architecture:** Datadog's mobile session replay is built on the Wireframe Reconstruction method. Their developer documentation provides a clear and detailed explanation of this process. The recorder scans the application's screen and breaks it down into a series of simple rectangles, or "wireframes," that represent UI elements like text, images, and shapes. This structured data is then transmitted efficiently, with only incremental updates sent as the UI changes, minimizing performance impact.2 **SwiftUI Capture Implementation & Technical Approach:** * **Instrumentation:** Datadog's support for SwiftUI is explicitly labeled as an "experimental" feature. To enable it, developers must pass a specific feature flag during the SDK initialization: `featureFlags: [.swiftui: true]`.5 This designation indicates that SwiftUI support is newer and may be less stable or feature-complete compared to their mature support for UIKit. * **Capture Mechanism:** The existing wireframe recorder has been extended to support SwiftUI views. The underlying mechanism remains the same: the SDK scans the view hierarchy from the background to the foreground, creating a wireframe for each visible element. The Datadog player then reconstructs the screen during playback by drawing these wireframes in the correct order.2 * **Privacy & Data Masking:** Datadog provides several global privacy levels that can be configured during SDK setup. These include options like `.maskAll` (which redacts all text and input fields), `.maskAllInputs`, and `.maskSensitiveInputs` (which masks common sensitive fields like passwords and emails). These privacy rules are applied universally during the wireframe capture process. The documentation does not mention any SwiftUI-specific masking APIs or modifiers, suggesting that privacy control is less granular than what some competitors offer.20 * **Limitations & Known Issues:** The most significant limitation is the "experimental" status of the SwiftUI support. This implies that developers using it may encounter bugs, performance inconsistencies, or incomplete capture of certain custom SwiftUI views. Organizations with mission-critical SwiftUI applications may be hesitant to rely on a feature that is not yet considered generally available. **Primary Developer Documentation Link:** `https://docs.datadoghq.com/real_user_monitoring/session_replay/mobile/setup_and_configuration/` 5 ### 4. Microsoft Clarity **Provider Snapshot:** Microsoft Clarity is a highly disruptive force in the analytics market. It offers a comprehensive session replay and heatmap tool that is completely free, with no traffic limits, data sampling, or hidden paid tiers. This value proposition makes it an extremely attractive option for businesses of all sizes, from small startups to large enterprises, and puts significant pressure on paid competitors.8 **Core Mobile Capture Architecture:** Clarity employs a high-fidelity Native View Hierarchy Capture method. The documentation asserts that its SDK "re-creates user mobile sessions with perfect accuracy," which positions it technologically alongside the premium, paid solutions.23 A key focus of its engineering is performance, with Clarity claiming to have the "lightest SDK on the market" to minimize impact on app size and performance.23 **SwiftUI Capture Implementation & Technical Approach:** * **Instrumentation:** The Clarity iOS SDK provides official, first-class support for SwiftUI in versions 3.0.0 and above. The initialization process is straightforward and is typically done within the SwiftUI `App`'s `init()` function, making it easy to integrate into modern iOS projects.7 * **Capture Mechanism:** The SDK is designed to capture the SwiftUI view hierarchy directly. Like other developer-focused tools, it acknowledges the unique challenges presented by the framework and provides specific APIs to address them. * **Privacy & Data Masking:** To handle privacy in SwiftUI, Clarity provides developers with specific view modifiers: `clarityMask()` to redact a view and `clarityUnmask()` to explicitly un-redact a child of a masked view. This approach is a direct admission that fully automatic masking in SwiftUI is unreliable and requires developer intervention for accuracy. A notable limitation is that there is no global setting to mask all input fields; the documentation states that the `clarityMask` modifier must be applied to each input field individually.7 * **Limitations & Known Issues:** The primary limitation is the 30-day data retention policy, which may be insufficient for teams needing to analyze long-term trends.9 On the implementation side, the requirement to manually mask every single input field represents a significant developer overhead and increases the risk of accidentally leaking sensitive data if a field is missed. A previous limitation requiring a `clarityFontNameHint` modifier for custom fonts has been deprecated in recent versions, indicating active development and improvement in text capture capabilities.7 **Primary Developer Documentation Link:** `https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/clarity/mobile-sdk/ios-sdk` 7 ### 5. Sentry **Provider Snapshot:** Sentry is a developer-first application monitoring platform specializing in error tracking and performance analysis. Session Replay is not its core product but a powerful supplementary feature. 
</context body>]", "type": "TEXT"}, "location": {"confluenceLocation": {"url": "https://newrelic.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/APPEXP/pages/4655743612/SwiftUI+-+Wireframe+vs.+screenshots"}, "type": "CONFLUENCE"}, "score": 0.56441647}

{"content": {"text": "[<context summary>
- The context belongs to this section: Sentry
- The context belongs to this subsection: SwiftUI Capture Implementation & Technical Approach
- Keywords: session replay, native view hierarchy, privacy, swiftui
- About the context: This snippet describes Sentry's approach to capturing user sessions in SwiftUI applications. Sentry uses a native view hierarchy capture method, which allows it to reconstruct the application's state rather than just recording a video. The SDK provides official support for SwiftUI, with the session replay integration directly integrated into the standard Sentry initialization process. However, the documentation lacks details on the specific technical mechanism for traversing and capturing the SwiftUI view hierarchy, and it is unclear how developers can achieve granular control over privacy masking in a pure SwiftUI context.
</context summary>

<context body>
Session Replay is not its core product but a powerful supplementary feature. Its primary use case is to provide immediate visual context for an error or crash report, allowing developers to see the exact sequence of user actions that led to the issue, thereby drastically reducing debugging time.19 **Core Mobile Capture Architecture:** Sentry uses a Native View Hierarchy Capture method. Their documentation clarifies that a replay is not a video or screen recording but a "reconstruction" of the application's state.12 This is analogous to how their web replay product reconstructs the DOM. A GitHub issue discussing the feature's development confirms that the engineering goal was to directly support native frameworks like UIKit and SwiftUI, not lower-level graphics APIs like Metal, aligning it with this high-fidelity approach.26 **SwiftUI Capture Implementation & Technical Approach:** * **Instrumentation:** Sentry provides official support for SwiftUI through its unified Sentry Cocoa SDK (requiring version 8.31.1 or higher).27 The setup for session replay is integrated directly into the standard Sentry initialization configuration block, making it simple for existing Sentry users to enable. * **Capture Mechanism:** The SDK captures the application's state, which includes screen transitions, user gestures like taps and swipes, network requests, and other telemetry.27 The public documentation does not offer a deep dive into the specific technical mechanism used to traverse and capture the SwiftUI view hierarchy, focusing more on the configuration and features. * **Privacy & Data Masking:** Sentry's SDK employs an aggressive-by-default privacy posture, masking all text and images automatically. Developers have the option to disable these defaults (e.g., `maskAllText = false`) or follow a guide for custom masking to achieve more granular control.27 The documentation snippets do not mention any SwiftUI-specific modifiers like those found in LogRocket or Clarity, which may suggest that privacy controls rely more on global rules or class-based redaction rather than per-view modifiers. * **Limitations & Known Issues:** A potential limitation is the apparent lack of SwiftUI-specific APIs for granular control over masking and tagging. While global rules are powerful, complex applications often require the ability to redact specific, individual views, and it is unclear from the documentation how this is achieved in a pure SwiftUI context. **Primary Developer Documentation Link:** `https://docs.sentry.io/platforms/apple/guides/ios/session-replay/` 27 ### 6. Dynatrace **Provider Snapshot:** Dynatrace is an enterprise-grade, all-in-one observability platform that heavily emphasizes its AI engine, "Davis." Session Replay is a feature within its Digital Experience Monitoring (DEM) suite. Its primary purpose is to feed qualitative, visual data into the AI engine, which then correlates user-facing issues with back-end performance problems to provide automated root cause analysis.8 **Core Mobile Capture Architecture:** Dynatrace describes its mobile replay as an Event-Based Reconstruction. The documentation states that it is a "video-like reconstruction of the user interactions with mobile applications that use captured events and data".30 This phrasing suggests a high-fidelity approach that is more sophisticated than simple wireframes, but it also comes with a crucial caveat that replayed sessions can differ from the actual user experience. **SwiftUI Capture Implementation & Technical Approach:** * **Instrumentation:** SwiftUI is officially supported through the Dynatrace OneAgent for iOS (version 8.323 and higher).30 The instrumentation process is guided by a wizard within the Dynatrace web application, which is typical for enterprise-focused platforms. * **Capture Mechanism:** While the documentation confirms that SwiftUI is a supported technology, it lacks deep technical specifics on the capture mechanism itself.30 The admission that replays "can differ from the actual user experience" is a significant qualifier, suggesting potential gaps in fidelity, especially with complex or custom SwiftUI components.30 * **Privacy & Data Masking:** Dynatrace offers a tiered approach to privacy with three predefined masking levels: "Safest," "Safe," and "Custom," which can be configured globally. For more granular control, developers can define custom masking rules that target views based on their `accessibilityIdentifier` or a specific masking tag.30 However, it is not clear from the available documentation whether these identifier-based methods work reliably with SwiftUI's unique view rendering. * **Limitations & Known Issues:** The most notable limitation is the explicit statement that replays may not be 100% accurate, which could be a deal-breaker for teams needing to debug subtle visual bugs.30 Additionally, Dynatrace Session Replay is not available for any cross-platform frameworks like React Native or Flutter. **Primary Developer Documentation Link:** `https://docs.dynatrace.com/docs/observe/digital-experience/session-replay/session-replay-ios` 30 ### 7. Glassbox **Provider Snapshot:** Glassbox is an enterprise-focused digital experience analytics platform, with a particularly strong presence in the financial services and insurance industries. A core feature of its platform is "tagless data capture," which automatically records 100% of user interactions and technical events without requiring manual instrumentation, providing a complete dataset for analysis.8 **Core Mobile Capture Architecture:** Glassbox utilizes a Native View Hierarchy Capture method. The platform is designed to automatically capture "every user interaction and technical event" from native mobile apps and deliver "video-like session replays".32 The company emphasizes its lightweight and mature SDK, which is engineered for high performance and stability in large-scale deployments. **SwiftUI Capture Implementation & Technical Approach:** * **Instrumentation:** The Glassbox documentation boasts high compatibility with a wide range of mobile frameworks, including iOS, Android, React Native, and even modern declarative frameworks like Jetpack Compose.33 While SwiftUI is not explicitly named in the provided marketing materials, its support is highly probable given the platform's enterprise focus and support for analogous technologies. * **Capture Mechanism:** The specific technical approach for capturing SwiftUI views is not detailed in the available research material.34 * **Privacy & Data Masking:** Glassbox heavily promotes its advanced and robust privacy controls, highlighting "patent-pending data masking" and omitting capabilities that give enterprises fine-grained control over what data is captured and who can access it.33 However, the specific APIs or methods for applying these controls within a SwiftUI application are not described in the provided snippets. * **Limitations & Known Issues:** The most significant finding from the research is the lack of specific, public-facing developer documentation regarding their SwiftUI implementation. For a prospective customer's engineering team, this information gap makes it difficult to evaluate the maturity and technical fit of the Glassbox solution for a modern SwiftUI application.34 **Primary Developer Documentation Link:** `https://www.glassbox.com/platform/mobile-app-analytics/` 33 (Note: This is a high-level product page. Specific, detailed developer documentation for SwiftUI was not found in the provided research.) ### 8. Contentsquare **Provider Snapshot:** Contentsquare is another major enterprise platform in the experience analytics space, competing directly with Glassbox. It focuses on providing a comprehensive suite of tools to understand and optimize customer journeys. A key feature is its AI engine, "Sense," which can automatically analyze and summarize session replays to surface the most impactful moments of user friction or engagement.8 **Core Mobile Capture Architecture:** Contentsquare employs a Native Screen Content Capture method. 
</context body>]", "type": "TEXT"}, "location": {"confluenceLocation": {"url": "https://newrelic.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/APPEXP/pages/4655743612/SwiftUI+-+Wireframe+vs.+screenshots"}, "type": "CONFLUENCE"}, "score": 0.554965}

{"content": {"text": "[<context summary>
- The context belongs to this section: Contentsquare
- The context belongs to this subsection: SwiftUI Capture Implementation & Technical Approach
- Keywords: SwiftUI, native rendering, screen capture, privacy, data volume
- About the context: This snippet provides details on how Contentsquare has implemented support for capturing user sessions within SwiftUI applications. It describes their native rendering approach, the techniques used for privacy masking, and the limitations they've encountered, including the fact that SwiftUI views generate significantly more data volume compared to traditional UIKit applications. This information is part of a broader competitive analysis of mobile session replay providers and their support for the SwiftUI framework.
</context summary>

<context body>
The SDK is designed to capture the rendered content of the screen, including text and images, to facilitate the replay.36 The platform's changelogs, which mention specific fixes for issues like missing SwiftUI images and incorrect gradient background rendering, confirm a deep, native integration that interacts directly with the framework's visual output.37 **SwiftUI Capture Implementation & Technical Approach:** * **Instrumentation:** The Contentsquare iOS SDK has explicit and mature support for SwiftUI. The product's changelog shows a history of continuous development, with updates specifically targeting SwiftUI bug fixes and feature enhancements, demonstrating an ongoing commitment to the framework.37 * **Capture Mechanism:** The SDK captures the rendered visual content of SwiftUI views. * **Privacy & Data Masking:** Contentsquare provides detailed documentation on its masking behavior for SwiftUI. By default, `SwiftUI.Image` views are replaced with a simple placeholder, and `SwiftUI.Text` views are replaced by a solid black rectangle.36 The documentation transparently addresses a key limitation of Apple's frameworks: system-level components like native SwiftUI `Alert`s and `Menu`s cannot be targeted by the masking API. The recommended workaround is to programmatically mask the entire application screen when these elements are presented and then unmask it when they are dismissed, which is a less-than-ideal solution.36 * **Limitations & Known Issues:** The inability to granularly mask native alerts and menus is a notable framework-imposed limitation that Contentsquare's documentation openly discusses. Another critical piece of information revealed is that, on average, screens built with SwiftUI produce 120% more data for session replay than equivalent screens built with UIKit. This suggests a potential for higher data transmission and storage costs for customers with SwiftUI-heavy applications.36 **Primary Developer Documentation Link:** `https://docs.contentsquare.com/en/ios/session-replay/` 36 ### 9. PostHog **Provider Snapshot:** PostHog is an open-source, all-in-one product analytics platform designed for engineers and technical teams. Its key differentiators include a transparent, open-core model, the option for self-hosting for maximum data control, and a generous free tier that makes it highly accessible. It bundles a wide range of tools, including event-based analytics, feature flags, A/B testing, and session replay, into a single platform.8 **Core Mobile Capture Architecture:** PostHog uses a hybrid approach. Its default capture method for mobile is Wireframe Reconstruction, where it uses native APIs to grab the view hierarchy and render it as an HTML wireframe for efficient, low-impact recording.10 However, it also offers an optional "screenshot mode." This mode provides higher visual fidelity but comes with the significant caveat that it may capture sensitive information if not masked carefully and will have a higher performance impact.10 **SwiftUI Capture Implementation & Technical Approach:** * **Instrumentation:** The PostHog iOS library documentation provides guidance for both UIKit and SwiftUI applications. For SwiftUI, it specifically recommends disabling automatic screen view capture and instead using a manual `.postHogScreenView()` modifier on each view to ensure that screens are tracked with meaningful, human-readable names.38 * **Capture Mechanism:** While a tutorial notes that the concepts for session replay are similar between UIKit and SwiftUI, community discussions in the documentation reveal a critical detail: to get session replay working for SwiftUI, developers are advised to enable `screenshotMode = true` in the SDK configuration.10 This strongly indicates that PostHog's primary and recommended capture method for the SwiftUI framework is the screenshot-based approach, not their default wireframing method. * **Privacy & Data Masking:** To prevent the capture of sensitive data, PostHog provides a simple mechanism. Developers can add the string `ph-no-capture` as either the `accessibilityLabel` or `accessibilityIdentifier` to any view. When the SDK detects this tag, it will either ignore or mask the element during capture.38 * **Limitations & Known Issues:** The reliance on screenshot mode for SwiftUI is a significant limitation. This approach carries all the inherent disadvantages of the method: larger data payloads, potential for choppy replays, and a higher burden on the developer to manually find and mask every sensitive view to avoid data leakage. This makes it a less performant and potentially less secure option compared to competitors with native rendering or wireframe support for SwiftUI. **Primary Developer Documentation Link:** `https://posthog.com/docs/session-replay/mobile` 10 and `https://posthog.com/docs/libraries/ios` 38 ### 10. UXCam **Provider Snapshot:** UXCam is a specialized mobile app analytics platform. Unlike many competitors who started on the web and later expanded to mobile, UXCam's focus has always been on native mobile applications. It positions itself as a leader in the mobile analytics space, offering a suite of tools including session replay, heatmaps, and user journey analysis designed specifically for mobile product teams.9 **Core Mobile Capture Architecture:** For traditional UIKit applications, UXCam's core technology is the Screenshot & Stitch method. Their documentation is transparent about this, explaining that replay is "based on timed screenshots captured by the SDK, which are encoded into a video".6 **SwiftUI Capture Implementation & Technical Approach:** * **Instrumentation:** UXCam's approach to SwiftUI is unique among the providers analyzed. They instruct developers to use a dedicated and separate **SwiftUI SDK** if their application is primarily built with SwiftUI. This is a significant departure from the unified SDK approach taken by nearly all other competitors.6 * **Capture Mechanism:** This is where UXCam reveals a critical technical distinction. When using the dedicated SwiftUI SDK, the documentation states that "native recording will be enabled".6 This indicates that UXCam has invested in a completely different, higher-fidelity capture method specifically for SwiftUI, moving beyond their legacy screenshot technology for the newer framework. This "native recording" is likely a form of the Native View Hierarchy Capture method. * **Privacy & Data Masking:** With the more powerful native recording mode comes a greater responsibility for the developer. The documentation warns that developers must "handle occlusion carefully".6 To facilitate this, UXCam provides a custom SwiftUI view modifier, `.uxcamOcclude()`, which can be used to hide sensitive views from the recording. An option `blockGestures: false` can be passed to still capture gestures on an occluded view.40 * **Limitations & Known Issues:** The primary challenge for developers is the need to choose between two different SDKs, which could create confusion or implementation complexity for teams maintaining applications with a mix of UIKit and SwiftUI code. The explicit warning to be diligent with manual occlusion for native recording highlights the trade-off for achieving higher fidelity. **Primary Developer Documentation Link:** `https://developer.uxcam.com/docs/swiftui` 41 ## Synthesis & Strategic Recommendations The detailed analysis of the top 10 mobile session replay providers reveals a market in a state of technological transition, driven by the architectural shifts introduced by SwiftUI. The varied approaches to capturing SwiftUI views reflect differing engineering priorities, target markets, and levels of investment in Apple's modern UI framework. 
</context body>]", "type": "TEXT"}, "location": {"confluenceLocation": {"url": "https://newrelic.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/APPEXP/pages/4655743612/SwiftUI+-+Wireframe+vs.+screenshots"}, "type": "CONFLUENCE"}, "score": 0.5544424}

{"content": {"text": "[<context summary>
- The context belongs to this section: CDD - Mobile session replay config settings
- The context belongs to this subsection: Design / Considerations and Consequences
- Keywords: Mobile session replay, configuration settings, effort to reverse, impact, risk level
- About the context: This snippet discusses the considerations and consequences of the design approach for mobile session replay configuration settings. It provides guidance on how to evaluate the effort, impact, and risk level of the proposed changes, covering aspects such as scalability, supportability, compliance, and trade-offs.
</context summary>

<context body>
For gauging the **Effort to Reverse**, consider the number of components to change in order to reverse, the effort to undo the breaking changes, are there one-way doors introduced with the change, how many teams would be involved in undoing the change, etc. For gauging the **Impact**, consider how many services/APIs/DBs are affected by the change (i.e., size of the scope of work), how many teams (both within your immediate org, and other orgs) are affected, whether breaking changes occur, requirements to learn a brand new technology or ecosystem, requirements for significant re-tooling, amount of brand new infrastructure required, cost to operate, etc. For **Risk Level** , refer to this table ## Context / Problem Statement {#CDD-Mobilesessionreplayconfigsettings-Context/ProblemStatement} Our users need a path to setting specific configuration settings for their mobile session replay data. Those configurations fall into three main categories. The first is enablement, the second is sampling and the third is PII masking options. The settings will be stored on the entity and retrieved via the mobile application settings endpoint in nerdgraph. The user will access their settings on the mobile application settings page. ## Design {#CDD-Mobilesessionreplayconfigsettings-Design} *High-level description of the design approach.* ### Considerations and Consequences {#CDD-Mobilesessionreplayconfigsettings-ConsiderationsandConsequences} *What's been considered as part of the design? What are the consequences of the design? Examples include:* * *SLA requirements and/or modifications to SLIs/SLOs* * *Scalability* * *Supportability* * *Compliance and security implications* * *Billing or usage metering implications* * *Short term vs long term product/platform vision* * *Any trade-offs that are being made, especially any that are one-way doors (decisions that can't easily be reversed)* * *Architectural constraints* * *What customers will see (both UI/UX changes, as well as UI performance)* * *Negative impacts to other teams* * Teams deploying apps in K8s either don't have strong visibility into instance types or are faced with a LOT of instance types with different trade-offs. Explore the resources in the FinOps portal](/wiki/spaces/CPE1/pages/3026780161/FinOps+-+Team+Pages) or contact [#help-finops for help. <br /> Session replay configuration options will reside in the current Mobile Application Settings UI. A separate "Session Replay" section will be created with a toggle to enable/disable session replay. The session replay option will default to ON for new mobile apps. Current mobile apps will default to OFF on release of the feature. Additional configuration options should be displayed for privacy controls of the customer's browser app. These options will provide the ability for the customer to define controls for which mobile elements should be blocked, ignored, or masked from the recordings. Additionally, the user needs options to specify the sampling rate for all sessions as a percentage of all sessions for that entityGuid. Additionally, we will give our users the ability to block specific versions of their app from storing data. #### Sampling: {#CDD-Mobilesessionreplayconfigsettings-Sampling:} Sampling will be configurable by customers by percentage of sessions that should record replays. In the future, we might want to be able to provide a configurable sample rate for sessions with crashes or ANRs, but that is not our first priority. Our sampling process should: * Include a replay "kill-switch" that will completely halt replay capture, regardless of sampling decisions * Create evenly distributed sampling (and have well understood biases) * Update in nearly real time (responding to customer configuration changes) #### PII and masking {#CDD-Mobilesessionreplayconfigsettings-PIIandmasking} We need to give the user the ability to add something to their code to indicate that they want us to mask text or render a placeholder. This masking happens in the agent, meaning that while serializing the component to rrweb json, the agent will replace the text with \* and add a placeholder for hidden views and images. This way, we never ingest the PII. What is the default masking behavior: * Masked: text, input text, images * Unmasked: views, user interactions How does this happen? 1. `nr-mask` and `nr-unmask`: Our users can add these tags to the accessibility identifier for iOS or the tag for Android for a component and it will mask or hide that component. This capability is slated for LP. 2. Custom tags: Our users might have tags or accessibility identifiers already in their code that correspond to text they want masked or images and views they want hidden. We will give the users the ability to share a list of those custom tags in the app settings page. This is slated for GA. 1. An example: An iOS app has a textfield that the user has previously added `socialSecurity` as the accessibility identifier. In the app settings page in New Relic, they add `socialSecurity` in the list of accessibility identifiers. Our agent masks that textfield 3. Custom classes: Our users might have custom classes already in their code that correspond to text they want masked or images and views they want hidden. We will give the users the ability to share a list of those custom classes in the app settings page. This is slated for [ADDRESS] or future state. 1. An example: An iOS app has an image class called `PuppyImageClass` that they want us to hide. In the app settings page in New Relic, they add `PuppyImageClass` in the list of classes. Our agent masks anything associated with that class. #### Rule Application and Priority {#CDD-Mobilesessionreplayconfigsettings-RuleApplicationandPriority} Rules passed down from the Config will have priority over those set locally in the agent. A view marked as being masked will cascade that setting to the rest of that section of the view tree. A view marked as being unmasked will NOT cascade that setting. It should only affect that direct component. A view marked as being unmasked will unmask that view if it is not directly referred to otherwise. For example, if a view is marked as being masked, but one of its children is marked as being unmasked, then the child view should be unmasked. The rest of the views of that parent view will continue adopting the setting of their parent. If a view has been directly told to be masked and unmasked, then it should be masked. For example, a view has its AccessibilityIdentifier contains `nr-unmasked`, but the class name is added to the mask list. The view should then be masked. Previous DACIs: 1. How are we masking PII in Mobile Session Replays 2. How should we specify application versions to block session replay? ##### Agent hard coded options: {#CDD-Mobilesessionreplayconfigsettings-Agenthardcodedoptions:} Give the client an api/extension on View/UIView which allows them to mark any particular view tree as not to be recorded. If this is used, all views in that hierarchy will not be recorded. 
</context body>]", "type": "TEXT"}, "location": {"confluenceLocation": {"url": "https://newrelic.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/APPEXP/pages/4037312678/CDD+-+Mobile+session+replay+config+settings"}, "type": "CONFLUENCE"}, "score": 0.5535366}

{"content": {"text": "[<context summary>
- The context belongs to this section: A Competitive Analysis of Mobile Session Replay Capture Methods for SwiftUI
- The context belongs to this subsection: Competitive Analysis: Top 10 Mobile Session Replay Providers
- Keywords: mobile session replay, SwiftUI, capture architecture, privacy, instrumentation
- About the context: This table provides a detailed comparison of how the top 10 mobile session replay providers have implemented support for Apple's SwiftUI framework. It covers their core capture architectures, instrumentation approaches, privacy/masking mechanisms, and known limitations. This information situates the table within the overall report, which is focused on analyzing the competitive landscape and technical capabilities of these providers for capturing user sessions in SwiftUI-based mobile applications.
</context summary>

<context body>
yzed providers. This matrix is designed to facilitate rapid assessment of the competitive landscape.

| | | | | | |
| - | - | - | - | - | - |
| **Provider** | **Core Mobile Capture Method** | **SwiftUI Instrumentation Approach** | **SwiftUI Privacy/Masking Mechanism** | **Key Limitations / Known Issues** | **Primary Documentation Link** |
| **LogRocket** | Native View Hierarchy | Unified iOS SDK; First-class support | Custom `lrHide()` modifier; Global `redactMenus` config | Incompatible with Xcode Previews; Modifiers must be applied inside view `body` | `https://docs.logrocket.com/reference/swiftui-about` 3 |
| **Fullstory** | Draw Instruction | Unified iOS SDK; "Automatic instrumentation" | On-device redaction before data transmission | Specific limitations not detailed in available documentation | `https://help.fullstory.com/hc/en-us/articles/360042772333-Getting-Started-with-iOS-Data-Capture` 18 |
| **Datadog** | Wireframe Reconstruction | Unified iOS SDK; Enabled via experimental feature flag | Global privacy levels (e.g., `.maskAll`, `.maskAllInputs`) | "Experimental" status implies potential instability or incomplete capture | `https://docs.datadoghq.com/real_user_monitoring/session_replay/mobile/setup_and_configuration/` 5 |
| **Microsoft Clarity** | Native View Hierarchy | Unified iOS SDK; First-class support | Custom `clarityMask()` and `clarityUnmask()` modifiers | 30-day data retention; No global masking for inputs (must be done individually) | `https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/clarity/mobile-sdk/ios-sdk` 7 |
| **Sentry** | Native View Hierarchy | Unified Cocoa SDK; First-class support | Aggressive default masking; Global rules (e.g., `maskAllText = false`) | Lack of documented SwiftUI-specific APIs for granular masking | `https://docs.sentry.io/platforms/apple/guides/ios/session-replay/` 27 |
| **Dynatrace** | Event-Based Reconstruction | Unified OneAgent for iOS; Official support | Global masking levels; Custom rules via `accessibilityIdentifier` | Replays may not be 100% accurate; Unclear if identifier-based masking works in SwiftUI | `https://docs.dynatrace.com/docs/observe/digital-experience/session-replay/session-replay-ios` 30 |
| **Glassbox** | Native View Hierarchy | Unified iOS SDK; Support is probable but not explicitly documented | Advanced, "patent-pending" masking capabilities | Lack of specific, public-facing developer documentation for SwiftUI | `https://www.glassbox.com/platform/mobile-app-analytics/` 33 |
| **Contentsquare** | Native Screen Content | Unified iOS SDK; Mature support | Default masking for `Text` and `Image`; API for custom rules | Cannot granularly mask native `Alert`s/`Menu`s; SwiftUI views generate 120% more data | `https://docs.contentsquare.com/en/ios/session-replay/` 36 |
| **PostHog** | Screenshot & Stitch (for SwiftUI) | Unified iOS SDK; Requires enabling `screenshotMode` | Manual masking via `ph-no-capture` accessibility identifier | Relies on lower-fidelity, higher-overhead screenshot method for SwiftUI | `https://posthog.com/docs/session-replay/mobile` 10 |
| **UXCam** | Native View Hierarchy (for SwiftUI) | Requires a separate, dedicated SwiftUI SDK | Custom `.uxcamOcclude()` modifier | Requires choosing the correct SDK for the app; Developer must handle occlusion carefully | `https://developer.uxcam.com/docs/swiftui` 41 |
</context body>]", "type": "TEXT"}, "location": {"confluenceLocation": {"url": "https://newrelic.atlassian.net/wiki/spaces/APPEXP/pages/4655743612/SwiftUI+-+Wireframe+vs.+screenshots"}, "type": "CONFLUENCE"}, "score": 0.55193657}
